point_001: The reported model sizes exclude the embedding and prediction layers, counting only the Transformer encoder. This makes compression ratios appear more impressive than they are in practice, since the full model (including embeddings) is required for deployment. The paper should clearly report and compare full model sizes.
point_002: Comparisons with baseline compression methods are limited. Despite naming many related works, only a few are included in experiments, and relevant methods such as NAS-BERT and sparsity-based approaches (e.g., structured/random sparsity) are missing from empirical comparisons.
point_003: No ablation comparing the different decomposition variants introduced in the paper (matrix decomposition, tensor train, Tucker) is provided. The final results appear to use only one variant (method IV), making it unclear whether the gains stem from the proposed approach or from other factors such as the distillation pipeline.
point_004: The tensor train decomposition is discussed as a candidate but excluded from experiments due to time constraints, leaving the comparison among decomposition strategies incomplete.
point_005: The rationale for jointly compressing all layers (both FFN and attention weights together) is not well justified. It is unclear why weights from structurally different components should be merged into a shared tensor, and why each FFN weight matrix is split into four parts.
point_006: Matrices across different layers may have different scales, which could undermine the validity of joint tensor decomposition (which minimizes a reconstruction error such as L2). The paper does not analyze whether matrix norms are comparable across layers or whether normalization would improve results.
point_007: Experiments are conducted only on BERT-base and TinyBERT. Larger models, where compression is arguably more critical and impactful, are not evaluated.
point_008: The paper does not report training or inference latency, making it impossible to assess the practical speedup. A 50× parameter reduction does not translate to a proportional throughput improvement, and this discrepancy is not explained.
point_009: The large accuracy drop observed when fine-tuning with knowledge distillation in certain ablation conditions (Table 6) is not adequately explained. The mechanism by which generalized distillation followed by task distillation recovers ~40% accuracy is not analyzed.
point_010: The novelty of the core idea is limited: Tucker decomposition for compressing neural network weights has been extensively studied for CNNs, RNNs, and embeddings, and prior work has already demonstrated low-rank structure and redundancy in Transformer MHA and FFN layers.
point_011: The paper contains minor typographical errors, including a duplicated word ('the the') in Section 5.1 and ambiguous use of 'the latter' twice in Section 6.2.
point_012: The offline pre-computation claim in Section 4.2 (terms not involving batch size or sequence length can be computed offline during inference) is insufficiently explained and needs clarification.
